section.title: The Sabbath Year and the Firstborn
section.sectionRationale: This chapter continues the statutes shaping Israel's distinctive social economy, legislating a periodic release of debts and servants and consecrating every firstborn animal to the LORD, so that generosity and God's ownership of all things were built into Israel's very calendar.
section.thesis: Moses legislates a seventh-year release of debts and Hebrew servants, commanding open-handed generosity toward the poor precisely because Israel would always have need among them, and consecrates every firstborn male of the flock and herd to the LORD as a reminder of His own claim on the firstborn.
section.frameNote: Four movements: canceling debts every seven years; opening your hand to the poor; freeing Hebrew servants generously; and the firstborn of the flock and herd set apart to the LORD.

verses.text: At the end of every seven years you must cancel debts.

This is the manner of {{shemittah|remission}}: Every creditor shall cancel what he has loaned to his neighbor. He is not to collect anything from his neighbor or brother, because the LORD's time of release has been proclaimed.
You may collect something from a foreigner, but you must forgive whatever your brother owes you.
There will be no poor among you, however, because the LORD will surely bless you in the land that the LORD your God is giving you to possess as an inheritance,
if only you obey the LORD your God and are careful to follow all these commandments I am giving you today.
When the LORD your God blesses you as He has promised, you will lend to many nations but borrow from none; you will rule over many nations but be ruled by none.

units.label: Open your hand to the poor
units.note: he will cry out to the LORD

verses.text: If there is a poor man among your brothers within any of the gates in the land that the LORD your God is giving you, then you are not to harden your heart or shut your hand from your poor brother.
Instead, you are to {{patach|open}} your hand to him and freely loan him whatever he needs.

Be careful not to harbor this wicked thought in your heart: “The seventh year, the year of release, is near,” so that you look upon your poor brother begrudgingly and give him nothing. He will cry out to the LORD against you, and you will be guilty of sin.
Give generously to him, and do not let your heart be grieved when you do so. And because of this the LORD your God will bless you in all your work and in everything to which you put your hand.
For there will never cease to be poor in the land; that is why I am commanding you to open wide your hand to your brother and to the poor and needy in your land.
If a fellow Hebrew, a man or a woman, is sold to you and serves you six years, then in the seventh year you must set him {{chophshi|free}}.
And when you release him, do not send him away empty-handed.
You are to furnish him liberally from your flock, your threshing floor, and your winepress. You shall give to him as the LORD your God has blessed you.
Remember that you were slaves in the land of Egypt, and the LORD your God redeemed you; that is why I am giving you this command today.
But if your servant says to you, ‘I do not want to leave you,’ because he loves you and your household and is well off with you,
then take an awl and pierce it through his ear into the door, and he will become your servant for life. And treat your maidservant the same way.
Do not regard it as a hardship to set your servant free, because his six years of service were worth twice the wages of a hired hand. And the LORD your God will bless you in all you do.

units.label: The firstborn set apart to the LORD

verses.text: You must set apart to the LORD your God every firstborn male produced by your herds and flocks. You are not to put the firstborn of your oxen to work, nor are you to shear the firstborn of your flock.
Each year you and your household are to eat it before the LORD your God in the place the LORD will choose.
But if an animal has a defect, is lame or blind, or has any serious flaw, you must not sacrifice it to the LORD your God.
Eat it within your gates; both the ceremonially unclean and clean may eat it as they would a gazelle or a deer.
But you must not eat the blood; pour it on the ground like water.

shemittah.gloss: Release, remission, a dropping or letting fall. Names the seventh-year cancellation of debts, a structural, built-in reset preventing poverty from becoming permanent and inescapable within the community.

patach.gloss: To open. Used of the hand opened wide toward the poor, an image of deliberate, generous, unclenched giving rather than the closed fist of a hardened heart.

chophshi.gloss: Free, released from servitude. Describes the mandatory release of a Hebrew servant after six years, grounded explicitly in Israel's own memory of being freed from slavery in Egypt.

basic.context: Moses establishes a seventh-year release of debts and Hebrew servants, commands open-handed generosity toward the poor because need would never fully disappear, and consecrates every firstborn male animal to the LORD as an ongoing reminder of His prior claim on all Israel possesses.
basic.hermeneutics: This is specific social and economic legislation for Israel's agrarian covenant community, a real structural safeguard against permanent poverty and servitude. Its underlying principles, generosity, periodic release from crushing debt, and recognizing God's prior claim on all we have, remain abiding truths even though its precise mechanisms belonged to Israel's particular society.
basic.originalLanguages: The shemittah, release, cancels debts structurally every seventh year, preventing poverty from calcifying into permanence. Israel is commanded to patach, open, their hand freely to the poor, and to set every servant chophshi, free, after six years, grounded explicitly in their own history as slaves redeemed from Egypt.
basic.typology: The sabbatical release of debts and servants foreshadows the ultimate release from the debt of sin accomplished in Christ, and the year of the LORD's release that Jesus announces as fulfilled in Himself, quoting Isaiah's jubilee language, Luke 4:18-19.
basic.christ: Jesus opens His ministry by announcing 'the year of the LORD's favor,' Luke 4:18-19, language rooted in the sabbatical and jubilee release traditions of texts like this one, proclaiming Himself the true fulfillment of release from every debt, especially the debt of sin.
basic.god: God structures an entire economic system around generosity and periodic release, refusing to let poverty or servitude become permanent, and He grounds Israel's own generosity in their memory of being redeemed from slavery, showing that grace received is meant to overflow into grace given.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 4:18-19 records Jesus announcing the year of the LORD's favor, echoing this passage's sabbatical release language. Mark 14:7 echoes verse 11's realism that the poor will always be present. 2 Corinthians 8:9 grounds Christian generosity in Christ's own self-giving. Matthew 18:23-35 parables the forgiveness of an enormous debt.
basic.oneStory: This chapter builds structural, recurring generosity into Israel's calendar, refusing to let poverty or servitude become permanent, and rooting all of it in the memory of their own redemption from slavery. That pattern of grace received overflowing into grace given anticipates and is fulfilled in Christ's own proclamation of ultimate release from the far greater debt of sin.

context.literary: This chapter follows the food and tithe laws of chapter 14 with legislation shaping Israel's broader economic life, debt, servitude, and firstborn dedication, all patterned around recurring cycles that regularly reset the community toward generosity and worship.
context.historical: In an agrarian society without modern banking or insurance, debt could easily become permanent and servitude hereditary; this legislation built structural relief directly into Israel's calendar, a genuinely countercultural provision compared to surrounding ancient Near Eastern economies.
context.canonical: The sabbatical release pattern here becomes part of the broader biblical jubilee tradition, Leviticus 25, later invoked by Jesus in Luke 4:18-19 as He announces His own ministry as the arrival of the ultimate year of the LORD's favor.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To build recurring, structural generosity and release into Israel's economic life, ensuring poverty and servitude did not become permanent, and to root that generosity explicitly in the community's own memory of being redeemed from slavery in Egypt.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific mechanisms, seven-year debt cancellation, six-year servant release, firstborn animal dedication, describe covenant legislation particular to Israel's agrarian society. What is prescriptive and abiding is the underlying principle: God's people must not harden their hearts against the poor, must practice recurring, structural generosity rather than occasional charity, and must recognize God's prior claim on all they possess.

debates.question: How does verse 4's promise, 'there will be no poor among you,' relate to verse 11's statement that 'there will never cease to be poor in the land'?

views.label: A conditional ideal followed by a realistic acknowledgment
views.case: Verse 4 describes what would be true if Israel obeyed perfectly and received full covenant blessing, while verse 11 realistically acknowledges that, given ongoing human sin and circumstance, poverty would in practice persist, grounding an ongoing command to generosity rather than a false hope that need would vanish.

views.label: Both verses together frame generosity as both an ideal and a lived reality
views.case: Some read these verses as intentionally held together: the ideal of no poverty motivates obedience, while the realistic acknowledgment of continuing poverty ensures the command to generosity remains urgent and permanent rather than becoming obsolete once the ideal seems unreached.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the passage never treats the presence of poverty as an excuse for stinginess; if anything, the ongoing reality of need makes structural, generous obedience more urgent, not less.

study.originalLanguages: The shemittah, release, structurally cancels debts every seventh year so poverty cannot calcify into a permanent condition. Israel must patach, open, their hand generously to the poor, and grant every servant chophshi, freedom, after six years, a pattern grounded explicitly in Israel's own history as those redeemed from slavery in Egypt.

typology.type: The sabbatical release of debts and servants
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the ultimate release from the debt of sin, announced by Christ as the arrival of 'the year of the LORD's favor,' Luke 4:18-19.

typology.type: The firstborn set apart to the LORD, belonging to Him alone
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows Christ, the true firstborn set apart and consecrated to God on behalf of His people, Colossians 1:15, 18.

study.christ: Jesus opens His public ministry in Luke 4:18-19 by quoting Isaiah's jubilee language and announcing 'the year of the LORD's favor,' directly rooted in the sabbatical and jubilee release traditions this chapter helps establish, proclaiming Himself the true and final fulfillment of release, not merely from financial debt but from the far greater debt of sin that no seven-year cycle could ever cancel.
study.god: God builds an entire economic rhythm around generosity and periodic release, refusing to let poverty or servitude calcify into permanence, and He grounds Israel's own obligation to generosity explicitly in their memory of being redeemed from slavery, showing that grace received is always meant to overflow into grace given to others.

crossReferences.note: Jesus announces the year of the LORD's favor, echoing the sabbatical release language rooted in passages like this one.
Echoes verse 11's realistic acknowledgment that the poor will always be present, calling for ongoing generosity.
Grounds Christian generosity in Christ's own self-giving, becoming poor so others might become rich.
Jesus' parable of the unforgiving servant, illustrating the far greater debt of sin that has been forgiven.

study.oneStory: This chapter builds structural, recurring generosity into Israel's calendar, refusing to let poverty or servitude become permanent, and rooting all of it in the memory of their own redemption from slavery in Egypt. That pattern of grace received overflowing into grace given anticipates and is fulfilled in Christ's proclamation of ultimate release from the far greater debt of sin.

quiz.q: What must every creditor do at the end of every seven years (vv.1-2)?

options.text: Cancel what he has loaned to his neighbor or brother, since the LORD's time of release has been proclaimed.
options.note: Verses 1-2 state this exact requirement.

options.text: Double the interest owed on the loan.
options.note: The command is cancellation, the opposite of increasing the debt.

options.text: Transfer the debt to the king's treasury.
options.note: No such transfer is mentioned; the debt is simply canceled.

options.text: Nothing is required of creditors.
options.note: Verses 1-2 give an explicit, specific requirement.

quiz.q: What warning does Moses give against withholding help from the poor as the seventh year approaches (v.9)?

options.text: Do not harbor the wicked thought of withholding a loan because the year of release is near, or the poor person's cry to the LORD will bring guilt on you.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this exact warning.

options.text: Lending near the seventh year is entirely forbidden.
options.note: The opposite is commanded; Israel is warned against refusing to lend, not instructed to stop lending.

options.text: The poor person should be reported to the elders instead of helped.
options.note: No such instruction appears; the command is direct, personal generosity.

options.text: No warning is given about this.
options.note: Verse 9 gives an explicit and pointed warning.

quiz.q: What must a master do when releasing a Hebrew servant after six years of service (vv.13-14)?

options.text: Not send him away empty-handed, but furnish him liberally from the flock, threshing floor, and winepress.
options.note: Verses 13-14 state this exact requirement.

options.text: Charge him a release fee before he can leave.
options.note: No such fee is mentioned; the master is instead required to give generously to the departing servant.

options.text: Keep all his previous wages as payment for room and board.
options.note: The text requires generous provision at release, not withholding of wages.

options.text: No instruction is given about how to release him.
options.note: Verses 13-14 give explicit instruction.

quiz.q: What could a servant do if he did not want to leave his master after six years (vv.16-17)?

options.text: Declare his love for his master and household, after which his ear would be pierced with an awl, making him a servant for life.
options.note: Verses 16-17 describe exactly this option and process.

options.text: He had no option but to leave regardless of his wishes.
options.note: Verses 16-17 explicitly describe a voluntary option to remain permanently.

options.text: He could only stay by paying a large fee.
options.note: No fee is mentioned; the process described is the ear-piercing ceremony, not a payment.

options.text: No such option is described.
options.note: Verses 16-17 describe this option in specific detail.

quiz.q: What is Israel commanded to do with the firstborn males of their herds and flocks (vv.19-20)?

options.text: Set them apart to the LORD, not working the firstborn ox or shearing the firstborn sheep, and eat them yearly before the LORD.
options.note: Verses 19-20 give this exact instruction.

options.text: Sell them immediately for maximum profit.
options.note: The instruction is dedication to the LORD, not commercial sale.

options.text: Destroy them completely as unclean.
options.note: The firstborn animals are eaten in a sacred meal before the LORD, not destroyed as unclean.

options.text: No instruction regarding firstborn animals is given.
options.note: Verses 19-20 give explicit, detailed instruction.

questions: God commanded a total, structural reset of debt every seven years, refusing to let poverty become permanent and inescapable. Where do you need to consider forgiving, releasing, or restructuring something owed to you, rather than holding it indefinitely?
Moses warns against the 'wicked thought' of withholding generosity simply because it is inconvenient timing. Where have you delayed genuine generosity while waiting for a more convenient moment?

A released servant was to be sent away 'liberally,' not empty-handed, ensuring a genuine fresh start, not merely technical freedom. Where might you be offering someone freedom in name but not truly equipping them for it in practice?

The firstborn of the flock belonged to the LORD as a reminder that everything ultimately belongs to Him. What first portion, first fruits, or best of what you have are you currently withholding from God?

Moses says 'there will never cease to be poor in the land' (v.11), grounding ongoing generosity in an honest view of the world rather than a naive hope that need will disappear. How does accepting that need will always exist shape a more sustained, rather than occasional, practice of generosity in your own life?

translationNotes.note: 'Remission' translates shemittah, literally 'a dropping' or 'letting fall,' the technical term for this structural cancellation of debt.
This verse's realism about ongoing poverty is echoed by Jesus in Mark 14:7, 'you always have the poor with you,' used there to sharpen rather than excuse the call to generosity.
The ear-piercing ceremony for a servant who chose to remain permanently symbolized a willing, ongoing attachment to the household, a vivid and binding public act rather than a mere legal formality.
